name: Recover and merge site changes
description: I just made some changes to my personal site and checked out master, but now I can't find those changes. Please help me find them and merge them into master.

goal.answer: Locate the user's recently-made changes to their personal site that disappeared after switching to master, and merge them into master without losing data.

criteria.answer:
Identify where the lost changes currently live (branch, stash, detached HEAD, or reflog entry) by inspecting git state.
Merge or replay the recovered changes into the master branch so the working tree contains them.
Confirm master HEAD includes the recovered commits/work and no changes are lost (verified via git log, git diff against pre-recovery state, and listing of working-tree files).
Report the recovery path used (which branch/stash/commit was the source) and the resulting master commit history.

constraints.answer: Operate only inside the existing /app/personal-site git repository; do not initialize a new repo., Preserve the user's original commits — prefer fast-forward merge or replay over destructive reset/hard-delete., Use git plumbing (reflog, stash list, fsck) for recovery before any destructive operations., Do not push to any remote or alter remotes; this is a local recovery task.

phases.answer: Locate lost changes, Merge changes into master, Verify and report

assumptions.answer: The user is currently on the local master branch with their work-in-progress commits missing from HEAD. The lost work is reachable via one of: a feature branch that was never deleted, a stash entry, a detached-HEAD reflog entry, or an uncommitted working tree from before checkout. We can run git commands directly inside /app/personal-site to investigate and recover. The user wants the changes merged (or rebased/cherry-picked) onto master, not merely located — recovery must end with the changes actually present on master's working tree.

phases.name: Locate lost changes
phases.goal: Inspect git state (reflog, branches, stashes, fsck) to find every place the user's edits might still exist and identify which source to recover from.
phases.description: Read-only git inspection of /app/personal-site to map where the user's recent work is.

steps.description: Snapshot current git state: status, HEAD, branches (local + remote), recent reflog, stash list, and uncommitted diff. Capture the SHA of master HEAD before any changes.
Inspect reflog, all branches, stash list, dangling commits (git fsck --lost-found), and any uncommitted working-tree changes to enumerate every candidate source for the lost work.
Compare candidate sources against master HEAD and identify which one contains the user's site changes; record the source (branch name, stash ref, or commit SHA) to merge.

phases.summary: Located the lost work. Pre-recovery master SHA: `d7d3e4ba9350f634d92d39ace2b471433ee57d50`. The user's changes are in dangling commit `650dba427e0a9dcd118f41a4c5e35c8017550a5a` ("Move to Stanford", parent `c4e38a1`), which is a sibling of master (share parent c4e38a1, neither is an ancestor of the other). It modifies `_includes/about.md` (role → Stanford postdoc) and `_layouts/default.html` (header/formatting). No stashes, no remotes, no untracked files. Integration plan: create `recover-stanford` branch at 650dba4, then `git merge --no-ff recover-stanford` into master, resolving the expected conflict in `_includes/about.md`.

grade.rationale: All gates pass; project checks validate.

phases.name: Merge changes into master
phases.goal: Replay the recovered work onto master using the safest available strategy (fast-forward, merge, or cherry-pick) so the changes appear on master without losing history.
phases.description: Apply the recovered source onto the current master branch.

steps.description: Confirm master is the active branch and working tree is clean before integrating recovered changes.

Integrate the recovered source into master. If a branch exists with the commits, fast-forward or no-ff merge it into master. If only a stash or detached commit exists, apply via stash pop or cherry-pick onto master. Use --ff-only when safe; fall back to --no-ff only when fast-forward is impossible.
Show the resulting diff between recovered work and the pre-recovery master SHA, plus file-level status, to confirm the changes are present.

phases.summary: Merged recovered work into master. Recovery branch `recover-stanford` was created at `650dba4` and merged into master with `git merge --no-ff -m "Recover 'Move to Stanford' from dangling commit 650dba4"`, producing merge commit `ffbccb021e2ed0452a80f7ab8b98424b1aa5e9ec`. The expected conflict in `_includes/about.md` was resolved by keeping the Stanford role from the recovered commit. Final master HEAD includes both the merge commit and the original `650dba4` ("Move to Stanford") commit; `recover-stanford` branch retained for traceability. Post-merge verification confirmed only the two expected files changed, content reflects the Stanford update, working tree clean, no files lost.

grade.rationale: All gates pass; project checks validate.

phases.name: Verify and report
phases.goal: Prove the recovery is complete: master contains the changes, working tree is clean, and no source of recovery was accidentally destroyed.
phases.description: Final verification and summary of what was recovered and how.

steps.description: Verify master HEAD includes the recovered commits, working tree matches HEAD, and the original recovery source (branch/stash) is preserved where appropriate.
Produce a final recovery report listing: pre-recovery master SHA, source of recovered work (branch/stash/SHA), strategy used, and the resulting master log.

phases.summary: Verification and reporting complete. master HEAD = `ffbccb021e2ed0452a80f7ab8b98424b1aa5e9ec`, working tree clean, recovered commits (`ffbccb0` merge + `650dba4` original) reachable from master. Final report covers pre-recovery SHA, source SHA, strategy (recover-stanford branch + --no-ff merge + conflict resolution), resulting log, file diffs, preserved recovery branch, and notes about unrelated dangling objects.

grade.rationale: All gates pass; project checks validate.

goal: Locate the user's recently-made changes to their personal site that disappeared after switching to master, and merge them into master without losing data.
successCriteria: Identify where the lost changes currently live (branch, stash, detached HEAD, or reflog entry) by inspecting git state.
Merge or replay the recovered changes into the master branch so the working tree contains them.
Confirm master HEAD includes the recovered commits/work and no changes are lost (verified via git log, git diff against pre-recovery state, and listing of working-tree files).
Report the recovery path used (which branch/stash/commit was the source) and the resulting master commit history.
constraints: Operate only inside the existing /app/personal-site git repository; do not initialize a new repo.
Preserve the user's original commits — prefer fast-forward merge or replay over destructive reset/hard-delete.
Use git plumbing (reflog, stash list, fsck) for recovery before any destructive operations.
Do not push to any remote or alter remotes; this is a local recovery task.

grade.rationale: Every phase delivered without retries, with real git verification (rev-parse, fsck, merge-base, actual git merge --no-ff, log, diff) cited at every F-gate. The diff cleanly implements the recovery: about.md now reads 'Postdoctoral Researcher at Stanford CS.' and default.html header reads 'Postdoc @ Stanford', matching the summary's claims. Gate rationales cite specific SHAs (650dba4, ffbccb0, d7d3e4b), specific files, and the conflict resolution in _includes/about.md is explicitly justified by user intent. Intentional exclusion of unrelated dangling commit b0d59cd is well-documented.
